citations.narrative:
Several areas of the facility needed repair and maintenance. All structures must be maintained in good repair to help
protect the enclosed animals.
-Near the hyena enclosure two areas of a border fence were damaged from trees falling onto the fence. The same section
of fencing had a large hole in the fence near the ground.
-A section of broken wire panel was lying on the ground near the first water crossing. Animals may become injured from
entrapment in the discarded panel section.
-Near the entrance to the camel enclosure, a section of mesh wire fencing was lying on the ground. A length of older
fencing was damaged having loose wires and multiple holes which could be a source of entrapment to the animals. A
metal fence post had been left inside the enclosure and could be a source of injury to the animals.
-The wire portion of the metal pipe enclosure fence for the male watusi had been damaged and needed repairs to prevent
potential injury to animals.
-An implement used to spread manure having sharp metal spikes had been left in an area where animals travel. Animals
may be injured by stepping onto the sharp spikes.
-Near a shelter structure used lumber materials had been left on the ground. The lumber had several exposed screws that
could cause injury to the animals.
-A section of enclosure fence across from the rhino pen had a large hole with broken wire panels. Some of the panels had
sharp points that could cause injury.
-The enclosure housing three nilgai had several metal posts extending approximately six feet in height. The metal fence
posts had been left in the enclosure after completion of fence repairs.
All structures must be maintained in good repair to help protect the enclosed animals. The indoor and outdoor housing
facilities shall be structurally sound and shall be maintained in good repair to protect the animals from injury and to
contain the animals.

- The male arctic wolf had a heavy concentration of flying insects hovering around his back and head. The facility had not
provided any form of pest control.
- The male white tiger had numerous flies on his ears. The tiger was constantly twitching his ears and shaking his head in
response to the pests. The facility did not provide any form of pest control to prevent the irritation being caused by the
pests.
Failure to control insects may lead to disease transmission and skin irritation. A safe and effective program for the control
of insects, ectoparasites, and avian and mammalian pests shall be established and maintained. The facility should contact
the attending veterinarian for guidance on proper and safe pest control.
To be corrected by 5-5-2026.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a facility representative.
Additional Inspectors:
MICHAEL TYGART, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n

narrative:
3.125(a)  Repeat
   
Facilities, general. 
Several areas of the facility needed repair and maintenance. All structures must be maintained in good repair to help 
protect the enclosed animals. 
-Near the hyena enclosure two areas of a border fence were damaged from trees falling onto the fence. The same section 
of fencing had a large hole in the fence near the ground. 
-A section of broken wire panel was lying on the ground near the first water crossing. Animals may become injured from

-Near the entrance to the camel enclosure, a section of mesh wire fencing was lying on the ground. A length of older 
fencing was damaged having loose wires and multiple holes which could be a source of entrapment to the animals. A 
metal fence post had been left inside the enclosure and could be a source of injury to the animals. 
-The wire portion of the metal pipe enclosure fence for the male watusi had been damaged and needed repairs to prevent

-An implement used to spread manure having sharp metal spikes had been left in an area where animals travel. Animals 
may be injured by stepping onto the sharp spikes. 
-Near a shelter structure used lumber materials had been left on the ground. The lumber had several exposed screws that 
could cause injury to the animals. 
-A section of enclosure fence across from the rhino pen had a large hole with broken wire panels. Some of the panels had

-The enclosure housing three nilgai had several metal posts extending approximately six feet in height. The metal fence

All structures must be maintained in good repair to help protect the enclosed animals. The indoor and outdoor housing 
facilities shall be structurally sound and shall be maintained in good repair to protect the animals from injury and to

3.131(d) 
   
Sanitation. 
- The male arctic wolf had a heavy concentration of flying insects hovering around his back and head. The facility had not 
provided any form of pest control. 
- The male white tiger had numerous flies on his ears. The tiger was constantly twitching his ears and shaking his head in

response to the pests. The facility did not provide any form of pest control to prevent the irritation being caused by the 
pests.  
Failure to control insects may lead to disease transmission and skin irritation. A safe and effective program for the control 
of insects, ectoparasites, and avian and mammalian pests shall be established and maintained. The facility should contact 
the attending veterinarian for guidance on proper and safe pest control. 
 
To be corrected by 5-5-2026. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a facility representative. 
 
Additional Inspectors: 
MICHAEL TYGART, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n

species.common: GRAY WOLF / GREY WOLF / TIMBER WOLF
